station.title: Chest discomfort after lunch
station.note: Original practice station aligned to the current AMC format. It is not an AMC examination station or official marking script.

lines.text: You are a general practitioner working in a suburban medical clinic.
Mr Daniel Morgan, a 60-year-old man, has attended after developing chest discomfort following lunch.
No observations or investigation results are available at the start of the consultation.

tasks.text: Explain your most likely diagnosis and important differential diagnoses to the patient.
Address the patient's concerns and explain your management plan.

essentialClues.text: Current exertional onset with preceding exertional episodes
Hypertension, dyslipidaemia, past smoking and premature family history

discriminators.text: Pressure is persistent and non-pleuritic, with no abrupt maximum at onset

redFlags.text: Ongoing exertional pressure with radiation and autonomic symptoms

safetyThresholds.text: Suspected acute coronary syndrome in primary care requires prompt transfer for definitive assessment
Oxygen saturation of 96% on room air does not require routine oxygen
Recent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use changes the safety of glyceryl trinitrate

criticalActions.text: Call Triple Zero for an ambulance promptly and provide monitored transfer
Begin continuous cardiac monitoring with defibrillator availability when ongoing ischaemic symptoms are identified, and obtain an ECG within 10 minutes without delaying transfer
Give aspirin 300 milligrams chewed promptly after excluding relevant contraindications
Give sublingual glyceryl trinitrate for ongoing pain when no contraindication is present, with continued symptom and blood-pressure monitoring

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: I am asking my colleague to call Triple Zero for an ambulance now.
criticalActionTimings.rationale: The complete cardiac pattern is apparent by the associated-symptom response, and current Australian guidance recommends prompt transfer from primary care.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: begins continuous cardiac monitoring and keeps the defibrillator ready
criticalActionTimings.rationale: Current Australian guidance recommends continuous monitoring and defibrillator availability during assessment when ischaemic symptoms are ongoing; ECG assessment remains required within 10 minutes without delaying transfer.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: The doctor gives Daniel aspirin 300 milligrams to chew
criticalActionTimings.rationale: Current Australian guidance recommends early aspirin 300 milligrams for suspected acute coronary syndrome after true anaphylaxis and bleeding disorder are excluded.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: The doctor gives one sublingual dose of glyceryl trinitrate
criticalActionTimings.rationale: Ongoing pain, safe blood pressure, no recent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use and no evident contraindication support a monitored sublingual dose for symptom relief.

unsafeActions.text: Do not attribute the presentation to indigestion before dangerous causes are assessed
Do not use one clinic troponin or an unsupported risk score to exclude acute coronary syndrome

scopeBoundary.text: The GP initiates assessment and treatment while arranging prompt transfer; definitive serial testing and cardiology management occur in hospital

initialVoluntary.text: About 35 minutes ago, after lunch, I was walking back to the office when I felt a heavy pressure in the middle of my chest. I sat down, but it has not gone away.

concerns.responseText: An ambulance? Is it really that serious?
I still thought it was indigestion. Could my wife drive me instead?

concerns.text: Whether a heart attack is definite
concerns.responseText: Does that mean I am definitely having a heart attack?

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about current persistence.
disclosures.responseText: Yes. It is still there.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about radiation.
disclosures.responseText: It goes into my left arm and a little into my jaw.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about associated symptoms.
disclosures.responseText: I feel sweaty and slightly sick. I am more short of breath than usual.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about syncope.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have not fainted.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about previous episodes.
disclosures.responseText: Twice this week I felt a milder pressure while hurrying. It settled after a few minutes when I stopped.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about time to maximum intensity.
disclosures.responseText: No. It built up over a few minutes.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about severe aspirin allergy.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have never had a severe reaction to aspirin.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about bleeding disorder.
disclosures.responseText: No, I do not have a bleeding disorder.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about haemoptysis.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have not coughed up blood.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about previous venous thromboembolism.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have never had a blood clot.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about pleuritic character.
disclosures.responseText: No, breathing does not change it.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about medical conditions.
disclosures.responseText: High blood pressure and high cholesterol.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about previous heart disease.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have not been diagnosed with heart disease.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about smoking history.
disclosures.responseText: I stopped five years ago. Before that, about a packet a day.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about premature family cardiac history.
disclosures.responseText: My father had a heart attack at 54.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about current medicines.
disclosures.responseText: Perindopril and atorvastatin. I took an antacid, but it made no difference.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about recent erectile-dysfunction medicine.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have not used any.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about understanding of the plan.
disclosures.responseText: You are worried it may be reduced blood flow to my heart. I need the ambulance and hospital tests even though it is not definite yet.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks the patient to identify the site.
disclosures.responseText: Right here, across the centre of my chest.
disclosures.responseUse: The central site leads to the radiation question.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor asks one focused question about current severity.
disclosures.responseText: About seven.
disclosures.responseUse: The current severity leads to associated-symptom assessment.

releases.requestDescription: The Doctor requests the current observations and examination findings.
releases.requestExactText: Please provide the current observations and examination findings.
releases.responseText: The airway is patent. The patient is alert, pale and clammy. Pulse is 96 beats per minute and regular, blood pressure is 148/88 mmHg in both arms, respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. Heart sounds are normal. The chest is clear with equal air entry. There is no chest wall tenderness, calf swelling or clinical heart failure.
releases.findingSource: The Examiner supplies standardised findings after the consented examination is represented.
releases.responseUse: The Doctor integrates the stable observations and examination into the diagnostic explanation.

releases.requestDescription: The Doctor requests an immediate ECG when available without delaying transfer.
releases.requestExactText: We will record a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes if available, without delaying the ambulance. A normal first ECG would not rule out acute coronary syndrome.
releases.responseText: The ambulance has been called. An ECG is available immediately and does not delay transfer. It shows horizontal ST-segment depression in leads V4 to V6.
releases.findingSource: The Examiner supplies the standardised ECG result after confirming that it is immediately available.
releases.responseUse: The Doctor interprets the ischaemic change and continues monitoring already begun for ongoing ischaemic symptoms.

scheduledPrompts.speaker: Examiner
scheduledPrompts.responseText: Four minutes have elapsed. Please proceed to the next task.
scheduledPrompts.markingConsequence: The Doctor proceeds to the remaining tasks; the prompt itself carries no performance inference.

closing.text: Thank you. The station is complete.

keySteps.description: Elicits the persistent exertional cardiac pattern and relevant dangerous alternatives
Recognises the danger threshold and calls an ambulance without waiting for certainty
Explains acute coronary syndrome as the leading diagnosis with important alternatives and uncertainty
Provides current evidence-based initial assessment and management

projectSafetyErrors.description: Dismisses persistent pressure as indigestion because it followed lunch
Waits for diagnostic certainty before ambulance transfer
Uses one clinic troponin or unsupported risk score to exclude acute coronary syndrome

globalRating.decisionRule: The global rating alone determines the station result: ratings 1 to 3 fail and ratings 4 to 7 pass.
globalRating.passStandard: Completes a focused history, recognises the dangerous cardiac pattern, explains uncertainty and provides safe initial management.
globalRating.failStandard: Misses the dangerous pattern, delays transfer, gives unsafe reassurance or substantially fails the assigned tasks.

identity.verificationText: Hello, I am Ushana, one of the doctors here. Could you confirm your full name and date of birth?
identity.rationale: The patient can directly confirm full name and date of birth before clinical questioning.

consents.requestText: Thank you, Daniel. Is it okay if I ask focused questions about the chest discomfort so I can keep you safe?
Daniel, may I examine you now?
Daniel, may I give you aspirin 300 milligrams to chew now?
Daniel, may I give one dose of glyceryl trinitrate under your tongue for the pain now?

informationRequests.requestText: Tell me what happened from the beginning.
informationRequests.responseText: About 35 minutes ago, after lunch, I was walking back to the office when I felt a heavy pressure in the middle of my chest. I sat down, but it has not gone away.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Is the pressure still there now?
informationRequests.responseText: Yes. It is still there.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Please point to where you feel it.
informationRequests.responseText: Right here, across the centre of my chest.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Does it travel anywhere else?
informationRequests.responseText: It goes into my left arm and a little into my jaw.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What number from zero to ten describes it now?
informationRequests.responseText: About seven.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What else have you noticed with it?
informationRequests.responseText: I feel sweaty and slightly sick. I am more short of breath than usual.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you fainted?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have not fainted.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you had anything like this before?
informationRequests.responseText: Twice this week I felt a milder pressure while hurrying. It settled after a few minutes when I stopped.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Did the discomfort reach maximum intensity immediately?
informationRequests.responseText: No. It built up over a few minutes.
responseEvidencePhrases: built up over a few minutes
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you ever had a severe allergic reaction to aspirin?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have never had a severe reaction to aspirin.
responseEvidencePhrases: never had a severe reaction to aspirin
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Do you have a bleeding disorder?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I do not have a bleeding disorder.
responseEvidencePhrases: do not have a bleeding disorder
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you coughed up blood?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have not coughed up blood.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you previously had a blood clot?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have never had a blood clot.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Is the discomfort worse when you breathe in?
informationRequests.responseText: No, breathing does not change it.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What medical conditions have you been treated for?
informationRequests.responseText: High blood pressure and high cholesterol.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Has a doctor diagnosed heart disease in the past?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have not been diagnosed with heart disease.
responseEvidencePhrases: not been diagnosed with heart disease
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Do you smoke?
informationRequests.responseText: I stopped five years ago. Before that, about a packet a day.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Any close family history of early heart disease?
informationRequests.responseText: My father had a heart attack at 54.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What medicines do you take?
informationRequests.responseText: Perindopril and atorvastatin. I took an antacid, but it made no difference.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you used any erectile-dysfunction medicine recently?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have not used any.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What do you understand about the plan?
informationRequests.responseText: You are worried it may be reduced blood flow to my heart. I need the ambulance and hospital tests even though it is not definite yet.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

variation.distinctJob: Recognise a persistent exertional cardiac pattern despite a meal-related indigestion explanation.
variation.newSurprise: Two milder exertional episodes emerge only after focused questioning.
variation.plannedTrap: The timing after lunch invites premature attribution to reflux.
antiFailureFeatures: Separate the patient's explanation from the symptom pattern.
Escalate once the dangerous pattern is established rather than waiting for test certainty.

duplicateCheck.conclusion: Case 1 trains acute coronary syndrome recognition with an indigestion attribution trap; Cases 2 to 4 separately train aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism and tension pneumothorax pathways.

actionDurations.exceptionRationale: The reviewed persistent cardiac pattern requires prompt ambulance activation before diagnostic certainty.

requiredEvidence.requirement: A focused history is visibly initiated
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Tell me what happened from the beginning.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Tell me what happened from the beginning.

requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: You have ongoing exertional central pressure
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: You have ongoing exertional central pressure spreading to your arm and jaw

requiredEvidence.requirement: The most likely diagnosis is explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: My most likely diagnosis is acute coronary syndrome
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: My most likely diagnosis is acute coronary syndrome

requiredEvidence.requirement: Important differential diagnoses are explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Important differential diagnoses include a tear in the main artery, a lung clot and a collapsed lung.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Important differential diagnoses include a tear in the main artery, a lung clot and a collapsed lung.

requiredEvidence.requirement: Prompt ambulance transfer is visibly confirmed
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: The ambulance is on its way.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: The ambulance is on its way.

requiredEvidence.requirement: The patient's concern about private transport is answered
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: An ambulance provides monitoring and treatment if you deteriorate.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: An ambulance provides monitoring and treatment if you deteriorate.

listenTest.notes: Timestamped spoken read-through placed the Examiner four-minute prompt immediately after run-summary at 240 seconds and the final Examiner line at 461 seconds. Before the prompt, 479 spoken words at 134.3 words per minute plus 26 seconds of observed Action time total 240 seconds. From the prompt onward, 492 spoken words at 157.0 words per minute plus 33 seconds of observed Action time total 221 seconds. Both segments remain within the audited natural-speech range.

sources.organisation: Australian and New Zealand Committee on Resuscitation
sources.title: Guideline 14.2 - Acute Coronary Syndromes: Initial Medical Therapy
sources.applicability: Initial oxygen, aspirin and nitrate therapy for suspected acute coronary syndrome.

claimMap.claim: Persistent exertional central pressure with arm or jaw radiation and autonomic symptoms is a high-concern pattern for acute coronary syndrome.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 26-27, initial assessment and history of presenting complaint

claimMap.claim: Time-critical alternatives to acute coronary syndrome include aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism and pneumothorax; reflux and musculoskeletal causes remain non-cardiac alternatives.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 26-27, targeted differential diagnoses and Table 2

claimMap.claim: Suspected acute coronary syndrome initially assessed in primary care requires prompt transfer to a facility able to perform definitive risk assessment.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 58-59, primary-care presentation recommendations and initial assessment

claimMap.claim: Ongoing ischaemic symptoms require continuous cardiac monitoring with defibrillator availability during assessment; suspected acute coronary syndrome also requires ECG assessment within 10 minutes and serial troponin-based assessment rather than a single unsupported primary-care rule-out test.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 7-8 and 58-59, ECG, monitoring, troponin and primary-care recommendations

claimMap.claim: A single contemporary point-of-care troponin result or unsupported primary-care risk score must not be used to exclude acute myocardial infarction or acute coronary syndrome.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, p. 59, troponin testing and primary-care risk assessment

claimMap.claim: Give aspirin 300 milligrams chewed or dissolved in suspected acute coronary syndrome unless contraindicated, after excluding true anaphylaxis and bleeding disorder.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, p. 7, initial therapeutic management

claimMap.claim: A person with suspected acute coronary syndrome and oxygen saturation of 96% on room air does not require routine oxygen.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, p. 8, initial therapeutic management

claimMap.claim: For ongoing chest pain, give sublingual glyceryl trinitrate every five minutes for up to three doses when no contraindication exists, with continued symptom and blood-pressure monitoring.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, p. 8, initial therapeutic management

claimMap.claim: The station uses the current AMC two-minute reading and eight-minute assessment format, predominant assessment area, observable tasks, key steps, domains and seven-point global rating threshold.

sourceLocators.locator: Clinical Examination Specifications V8.3, pp. 10-15 and 20, marking, timing and task-performance sections
Assessment Domains V1.1, domain definitions and performance expectations
